summary: This excerpt covers biographical details of Friedrich Nietzsche's life, a list of his major works, and a brief explanation of his views on perspectivism and value creation from Thus Spoke Zarathustra.

principles.statement: Greatness comes from the act of valuing, not the specific content of the beliefs.
principles.rationale: The collective will to see values come to pass and the willingness to strive are more essential than the objective merit of the goal itself.
principles.source_quote: Nietzsche asserted that what made people great was not the content of their beliefs, but the act of valuing.

mental_models.name: Table of Values
mental_models.description: A metaphor for the overarching goals and esteemed beliefs that guide a person or a community. The act of creating this table is what unites different peoples, even if the specific values differ.
mental_models.example: A table of values hangs above every great person, representing their act of esteeming.

signature_quotes.text: A thousand goals have there been so far, for there are a thousand peoples. Only the yoke for the thousand necks is still lacking: the one goal is lacking. Humanity still has no goal.
signature_quotes.context: From Thus Spoke Zarathustra, discussing the multitude of human values and the lack of a unified goal for humanity.

anti_patterns.description: Judging greatness solely by the objective merit or content of a goal.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Because the act of esteeming and the willingness to strive are what actually make people great, not the specific content of their beliefs.
